heading: TONUS SEXTUS — Christus est author, et finis fidei (Caput I)
card_summary: This chunk opens the sixth tone (Tonus Sextus) of De Harmonia Mundi with a chapter titled "Christus est author, et finis fidei" (Christ is the author and end of faith). Zorzi draws on Paul's Epistle to the Hebrews to establish Christ as both origin and consummation of all articles of Christian belief. He distinguishes between theological truths accessible to natural reason — such as God's existence and role as creator, which even Aristotle approaches from sense experience — and those truths concerning the Incarnate Word that can only be known through sacred Scripture. Zorzi then enumerates all twelve articles of the Apostles' Creed, demonstrating that each one refers ultimately back to Christ, supporting the position of certain contemporary theologians that Christ is the proper subject of all Christian theology.
essay:
## Overview and Context

Chunk 78 opens the *Tonus Sextus*, the sixth major tonal division of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The chapter heading — *Christus est author, et finis fidei* ("Christ is the author and end of faith") — signals a turn from the cosmological and kabbalistic harmonics of the earlier tones toward a more explicitly Christological and theological register. The source pages (1738–1739 of the Campanini edition) present the opening argument of what will be an extended meditation on Christ as the organizing centre of Christian revelation.

## The Pauline Foundation: Christ as Author and Consummator of Faith

Zorzi opens by citing Paul's Epistle to the Hebrews as his warrant for the claim that Christ is both the *author* and *consummator* of faith. He is explicit about his epistemic caution: he would not dare to assert this himself, but Paul's authority licenses it:

> Tot videntur credenda, quae non sunt Christus IESUS, quod nullatenus dicere auderem ipsum authorem, et finem fidei, nisi id me doceret Paulus, in cuius pectore ille resonat.

<cite>p. 1738</cite>

The passage from Hebrews that Zorzi quotes reads:

> Occurramus in oppositum nobis certamen suspicientes in authorem fidei, et consummatorem IESUM.

<cite>p. 1738 (cf. Hebrews 12:1–2)</cite>

This is the Vulgate rendering of the injunction to "run the race set before us, looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith." By placing this verse at the head of the sixth tone, Zorzi frames the entire section as an extended gloss on this Pauline text.

## Natural Reason, Aristotle, and the Limits of Philosophy

A central methodological argument emerges here: Zorzi distinguishes between two classes of theological truth according to the mode of access available to human reason.

First, there are truths accessible through natural discourse (*naturali discursu*): the existence of God and God's role as maker of all things (*omnium opifex*). Even Aristotle, proceeding as a physicist (*tamquam physicus*) from sense data (*per sensata*), does not ultimately deviate from other wise men who acknowledge God as craftsman of the whole world — even if Aristotle's method of ascending from sensory experience prevents him from fully affirming creation:

> Deum enim esse naturali discursu indagare possumus, et eundem omnium opificem fuisse, quamvis Aristoteles tamquam physicus, et per sensata procedens hoc non admittat. Experrectus tamen a sensatis ipsis, non discrepat ab aliis sapientibus, qui fatentur Deum mundi totius opificem.

<cite>p. 1738</cite>

This is a characteristic Zorzi move: rehabilitating Aristotle within a broadly Neoplatonic-Platonic framework, acknowledging the philosopher's limitations while insisting on a deeper convergence with the tradition of *sapientes* who recognize divine creative causality.

Second — and crucially — there are truths that are *only* available through sacred Scripture (*sacra eloquia*): specifically, the doctrine that God accomplished all things through the Incarnate Word (*verbum incarnatum*) who is Christ Jesus. This places Christology beyond the reach of natural philosophy and makes it a matter of pure revelation.

## Christ as Subject of All Theology

Zorzi reports — with evident sympathy — the position of certain recent (*novitiores*) theologians who held that Christ is the proper *subiectum* around which all of Christian theology revolves, since all articles of faith appear to refer ultimately to him:

> adeo, ut quidam de novitioribus theologis asseruerint Christum esse subiectum, circa quod versatur tota nostra theologia, cum omnes articuli ad ipsum Christum referri videantur.

<cite>p. 1738–1739</cite>

This position — associated in scholastic theology with thinkers in the Franciscan tradition, and harmonizing with the Scotist emphasis on Christ as the primary purpose of creation — fits naturally within Zorzi's broader Franciscan intellectual milieu and his Christocentric cosmology.

## The Apostles' Creed: A Christological Enumeration

Zorzi substantiates the claim that all articles of faith refer to Christ by running through the Apostles' Creed article by article. The enumeration is notably systematic:

- **First article**: "Credo in Deum Patrem" — the Father cannot be Father without the Son, who is Christ Jesus.
- **Second**: "Et in IESUM Christum filium eius unicum dominum nostrum" — explicitly Christological.
- **Third**: "Qui conceptus est de Spiritu sancto, natus ex Maria virgine."
- **Fourth**: "Passus sub Pontio Pilato, crucifixus, mortuus, et sepultus est."
- **Fifth**: "Descendit ad inferos."
- **Sixth**: "Tertia die resurrexit a mortuis."
- **Seventh**: "Et ascendit in coelum, sedet ad dexteram Patris."
- **Eighth**: "Inde venturus est iudicare vivos et mortuos."
- **Ninth**: "Credo in Spiritum sanctum" — Zorzi notes that the Spirit proceeds from both Father and Son (*Filioque*), and therefore cannot be understood apart from the Son.
- **Tenth**: The text breaks off at "Sanctam ecclesiam ca-" (evidently "catholicam"), leaving the enumeration incomplete at this page boundary.

The *Filioque* insertion is significant: Zorzi here aligns himself firmly with Western orthodox pneumatology against the Eastern position, and uses it precisely to reinforce Christ's centrality — even the article on the Holy Spirit cannot be grasped without reference to the Son.

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

The opening of the *Tonus Sextus* marks a pivotal moment in the architecture of *De Harmonia Mundi*. Having established in earlier tones the harmonic ordering of the cosmos through number, proportion, the Kabbalistic sefirot, and Neoplatonic emanation, Zorzi now moves to anchor the entire structure in Christology. Christ as *author et finis fidei* is not merely a theological proposition but the cosmological keystone: the Incarnate Word who, in Zorzi's synthesis, is identified with the creative Logos through whom all things were made. The argument that even Aristotle's cosmology converges with the tradition that recognizes God as *opifex mundi* prepares the reader for the further claim that the Incarnation is the culmination and fulfilment of everything the natural philosophers pointed toward, knowable only through revelation. The systematic review of the Creed translates Zorzi's harmonic cosmology into creedal, catechetical form.
